events.title: Pachacuti Founds Tahuantinsuyo
events.detail: Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui, ninth Sapa Inca, defeats the rival Chanka confederation near Cusco and launches an unprecedented imperial expansion. He reorganizes the Inca state into the four suyus (Tahuantinsuyo — 'Land of the Four Quarters'), constructs monumental architecture in Cusco, and begins transforming a regional chiefdom into the largest pre-Columbian empire in the Americas, eventually stretching over 4,000 km along the Andes.

sources.name: John Rowe, 'Inca Culture at the Time of the Spanish Conquest' (1946)
Gary Urton, 'Signs of the Inka Khipu' (2003)

events.title: Construction of Machu Picchu
events.detail: Pachacuti orders the construction of the royal estate later known as Machu Picchu on a ridge 2,430 m above sea level in the Urubamba Valley. The site encompasses temples, royal residences, terraced agriculture, and an astronomical observatory. Built without mortar using precisely fitted stone, it remains one of the world's greatest architectural achievements. UNESCO designated it a World Heritage Site in 1983.

sources.name: Johan Reinhard, 'Machu Picchu: Exploring an Ancient Sacred Center' (2007)

events.title: Tupac Inca Yupanqui's Military Campaigns
events.detail: Crown prince Tupac Inca Yupanqui leads massive military campaigns that extend Tahuantinsuyo south into present-day Bolivia, Chile, and Argentina, and north into Ecuador and Colombia. His forces reach the Maule River in Chile — the southernmost extent of Inca expansion. He later inherits the throne (c. 1471) and continues building the empire's road network (Qhapaq Ñan) and expanding the mit'a (state labor) system.

events.title: Huayna Capac Inherits the Empire
events.detail: Huayna Capac becomes Sapa Inca and expands the empire to its greatest territorial extent. He campaigns in Ecuador (Quito), establishing a northern capital. Near the end of his reign, reports reach him of strange men arriving by sea on the Pacific coast. He dies c. 1527, likely from smallpox — an epidemic spreading ahead of Spanish explorers — triggering a catastrophic succession crisis between his sons Huascar (Cusco) and Atahualpa (Quito).

sources.name: Pedro Cieza de León, 'Crónica del Perú' (1553)

events.title: Inca Civil War: Huascar vs. Atahualpa
events.detail: The death of Huayna Capac sparks a five-year civil war between his sons Huascar (backed by Cusco nobility) and Atahualpa (backed by northern army). Atahualpa's general Quizquiz defeats and captures Huascar near Cusco in 1532. The war severely weakens the empire — killing thousands and fracturing loyalties — just as Francisco Pizarro's expedition arrives on the Peruvian coast, dramatically amplifying the Spanish advantage.

sources.name: Garcilaso de la Vega, 'Comentarios Reales de los Incas' (1609)
Nathan Wachtel, 'The Vision of the Vanquished' (1977)

events.title: Pizarro Reaches the Peruvian Coast
events.detail: Francisco Pizarro, with roughly 168 soldiers, 62 horsemen, and limited artillery, arrives at Tumbes on the Peruvian coast in May 1532 after three voyages. He hears of the civil war between Huascar and Atahualpa and exploits the crisis. Moving inland, his force — outnumbered hundreds to one — marches toward the highland city of Cajamarca where Atahualpa is camped with an estimated 40,000–80,000 troops.

sources.name: John Hemming, 'The Conquest of the Incas' (1970)

events.title: Ambush at Cajamarca — Atahualpa Captured
events.detail: On November 16, 1532, Pizarro springs an ambush in the plaza of Cajamarca. After a brief address by friar Vicente de Valverde demanding Atahualpa's submission to the Pope and the Spanish Crown, the Inca rejects the Requerimiento. Pizarro's men open fire with arquebuses, charge with cavalry, and kill an estimated 2,000–6,000 Inca warriors in hours. Atahualpa is captured alive — a stunning Spanish victory achieved without a single Spanish fatality.

sources.name: John Hemming, 'The Conquest of the Incas' (1970)
Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala, 'Nueva Corónica y Buen Gobierno' (c. 1615)

events.title: Execution of Atahualpa
events.detail: Despite paying a ransom that filled a room with gold (estimated at 6 tons) and a second room with silver twice over, Atahualpa is tried on fabricated charges by Pizarro's men. On July 26, 1533, he is executed by garrote at Cajamarca after agreeing to Christian baptism. His death decapitates the empire's command structure. Spanish forces march on Cusco, looting the Coricancha (Temple of the Sun) and installing puppet Incas, formally ending the Tahuantinsuyo as an independent state.

sources.name: John Hemming, 'The Conquest of the Incas' (1970)

events.title: Francisco Pizarro Founds Lima
events.detail: On January 18, 1535, Pizarro founds the 'Ciudad de los Reyes' (City of Kings) on the left bank of the Rímac River near the Pacific coast. Chosen for its mild climate, river access, and proximity to the port of Callao, Lima becomes the administrative capital of the Viceroyalty of Peru (established 1542). It will dominate South American colonial commerce for over 250 years and remain Peru's capital to the present day.

events.title: Manco Inca's Great Siege of Cusco
events.detail: Manco Inca Yupanqui, initially a Spanish puppet ruler, escapes captivity and raises an indigenous army estimated at 100,000–200,000 warriors. Between 1536 and 1537, he besieges Cusco and nearly drives the Spanish out of the highlands. The fortified hill of Sacsayhuamán is the decisive battle site; Spanish cavalry and indigenous auxiliaries repel the assault. Manco retreats to the jungle stronghold of Vilcabamba, where his successors resist until 1572, when Tupac Amaru I is captured and executed.

sources.name: John Hemming, 'The Conquest of the Incas' (1970)
Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala, 'Nueva Corónica y Buen Gobierno' (c. 1615)

events.title: Viceroyalty of Peru Established
events.detail: The Spanish Crown establishes the Viceroyalty of Peru on November 20, 1542, by the New Laws of the Indies — the first comprehensive legislation to protect indigenous people from the worst abuses of the encomienda system. Lima becomes the seat of the viceroy, the audiencia (high court), and later the Inquisition (1570). The Viceroyalty initially encompasses most of Spanish South America. Silver from Potosí (in modern Bolivia) and mercury from Huancavelica fuel colonial prosperity for two centuries.

sources.name: Archivo General de Indias, New Laws of the Indies (1542)
Kenneth Andrien, 'Andean Worlds: Indigenous History, Culture and Consciousness' (2001)

events.title: Viceroy Toledo Executes Tupac Amaru I
events.detail: Viceroy Francisco de Toledo captures Tupac Amaru I, the last ruler of the Inca Neo-state at Vilcabamba, and has him publicly beheaded in Cusco's Plaza Mayor on September 24, 1572. The execution shocks even many Spaniards; Toledo is later criticized by King Philip II for the act. Tupac Amaru's martyrdom becomes a permanent symbol of indigenous resistance, inspiring the 18th-century rebel Tupac Amaru II and the 20th-century guerrilla organization MRTA.

sources.name: John Hemming, 'The Conquest of the Incas' (1970)
Arquivo General de Indias, Toledo correspondence

events.title: Inca Garcilaso Publishes 'Comentarios Reales'
events.detail: Gómez Suárez de Figueroa — known as Inca Garcilaso de la Vega — publishes Part I of the Comentarios Reales de los Incas in Lisbon. The son of a Spanish conquistador and an Inca princess, he is the first major mestizo intellectual. His work is the most comprehensive early account of Inca history, customs, and cosmology, and profoundly shapes European and later Peruvian national identity. Part II (Historia General del Perú) appears posthumously in 1617.

sources.name: Inca Garcilaso de la Vega, 'Comentarios Reales de los Incas' (1609)
Margarita Zamora, 'Language, Authority and Indigenous History' (1988)

media.source: Wikipedia / Inca Garcilaso de la Vega

events.title: Tupac Amaru II Launches Great Andean Rebellion
events.detail: José Gabriel Condorcanqui — who adopts the name Tupac Amaru II — leads the largest indigenous uprising in colonial American history, beginning November 4, 1780, with the capture and execution of the corrupt corregidor Antonio de Arriaga. The rebellion spreads across the southern Andes from Cusco to present-day Bolivia and Argentina. At its height, his forces may number 60,000–100,000. Spanish royalist forces suffer major defeats before rallying. By early 1781, Tupac Amaru is captured.

media.source: Wikipedia / Rebellion of T%C3%BApac Amaru II

events.title: Brutal Execution of Tupac Amaru II
events.detail: On May 18, 1781, Tupac Amaru II, his wife Micaela Bastidas, and other family members are executed in Cusco's Plaza Mayor. The Spanish attempt to quarter him by tying his limbs to four horses; unable to tear his body apart, they behead him instead. The rebellion continues under his cousin Diego Cristóbal Tupac Amaru until 1783. The total death toll from the uprising exceeds 100,000. In response, Spanish authorities ban Quechua-language books, Inca dress, and imagery — a systematic cultural suppression.

events.title: Cusco Rebellion of 1814
events.detail: The Pumacahua Rebellion (1814–1815), led by Mateo Pumacahua — an elderly cacique and former royalist — and the Angulo brothers, briefly establishes insurgent control over Cusco, Arequipa, and parts of the south. It is the most significant pre-independence uprising in Peru and anticipates many themes of the independence movement: creole-indigenous alliance, anti-colonial taxation grievances, and constitutional demands. Royalist forces suppress it within a year; Pumacahua is executed in March 1815.

sources.name: Timothy Anna, 'The Fall of the Royal Government in Peru' (1979)

events.title: San Martín's Expedition Lands at Paracas
events.detail: General José de San Martín, liberator of Argentina and Chile, crosses the Pacific with the Expedición Libertadora — 4,500 troops aboard a fleet commanded by Lord Cochrane. He lands at the Bay of Paracas on September 8, 1820, and sets up headquarters at Pisco. Rather than frontal assault, San Martín employs a strategy of political persuasion and guerrilla harassment, seeking to erode royalist support and provoke defections. Lord Cochrane seizes the Spanish flagship Mercedes, dramatically shifting naval power.

sources.name: John Lynch, 'San Martín: Argentine Soldier, American Hero' (2009)
Archivo General de la Nación, Peru

events.title: Peru Declares Independence
events.detail: On July 28, 1821, General José de San Martín proclaims Peruvian independence in Lima's Plaza Mayor before a large crowd, uttering the phrase: 'From this moment on, Peru is free and independent, by the general will of the people and by the justice of its cause.' He assumes the title of Protector of Peru. Independence is not yet militarily secured — Spanish royalist forces remain entrenched in the highlands — but the proclamation is a decisive political act that sets in motion the final campaigns.

sources.name: Archivo General de la Nación, Peru — Act of Independence

events.title: Battle of Junín
events.detail: On August 6, 1824, Simón Bolívar's forces — under field command of General Antonio José de Sucre — defeat a larger royalist cavalry force at the Battle of Junín on the Junín Plateau at 4,100 m altitude. Fought almost entirely with sabers and lances in a battle lasting less than an hour, it breaks the royalists' control of the central highlands and paves the way for the decisive confrontation four months later at Ayacucho.

events.title: Battle of Ayacucho — Final Spanish Defeat
events.detail: On December 9, 1824, General Antonio José de Sucre defeats Viceroy José de la Serna's royalist army of approximately 9,300 troops on the Pampa de Quinua near Ayacucho. The capitulation signed that evening formally ends Spanish military power in South America. Serna becomes the last Spanish viceroy and is taken prisoner. The battle seals the independence not only of Peru but of the entire continent. Ayacucho remains Peru's most symbolically significant military event.

sources.name: Archivo General de la Nación, Capitulation of Ayacucho (1824)

events.title: Bolívar Leaves Peru; Era of Caudillo Rule Begins
events.detail: Simón Bolívar departs Peru in September 1826, ending his tenure as dictator. His departure triggers decades of political instability — Peru has 35 presidents in its first 40 years of independence, most removed by military coups (cuartelazos). The era of caudillos — military strongmen who rule by force — dominates Peruvian politics until the 1870s, repeatedly interrupting attempts at constitutional democracy.

sources.name: Jorge Basadre, 'Historia de la República del Perú' (1964)

events.title: Guano Boom Transforms the Peruvian Economy
events.detail: Beginning in the early 1840s, Peru becomes the world's principal supplier of guano (seabird excrement) from the Chincha Islands — a high-nitrogen fertilizer crucial to European agriculture before synthetic fertilizers. Export revenue reaches 600% of government revenue by the 1850s. Rather than industrializing, Peru's elite uses guano income to service debts, fund imports, and enrich Lima's creole oligarchy. The resource curse squanders an extraordinary opportunity; by the late 1870s, the guano deposits are nearly exhausted.

sources.name: Shane Hunt, 'Growth and Guano in 19th-Century Peru' (1973)

events.title: Manuel Pardo: Peru's First Civilian President
events.detail: Manuel Pardo y Lavalle, founder of the Partido Civil (Civilistas), wins the 1872 election — the first time a civilian defeats a military candidate for the Peruvian presidency. His government attempts to build national infrastructure, reform education, and reduce the military's political role. However, he faces a collapsing guano economy, Chile's growing ambitions in the saltpeter-rich Atacama, and opposition from the Lima oligarchy. His party remains dominant until 1919.

sources.name: Jorge Basadre, 'Historia de la República del Perú' (1964)
Archivo General de la Nación, Peru

events.title: War of the Pacific Begins
events.detail: Chile declares war on Bolivia on April 5, 1879, after Bolivia raises taxes on Chilean nitrate companies in the Atacama. Peru, bound by a secret mutual defense treaty with Bolivia (1873), is drawn into the war. The conflict is fundamentally about control of the world's largest nitrate deposits in the Atacama Desert. Chile fields a modernized army and navy; Peru and Bolivia are militarily inferior and politically divided. The war will devastate Peru for a generation.

sources.name: William Sater, 'Andean Tragedy: Fighting the War of the Pacific' (2007)
Archivo del Ministerio de Relaciones Exteriores, Peru

events.title: Battle of Iquique — Miguel Grau's Huáscar
events.detail: On May 21, 1879, the Peruvian ironclad Huáscar, under Admiral Miguel Grau Seminario, defeats a Chilean wooden corvette (the Esmeralda) in the harbor of Iquique, killing its commander. The Esmeralda is sunk; the battle becomes a Chilean national tragedy. Grau subsequently harasses Chilean supply lines for months before being killed at the Battle of Angamos (October 8, 1879) when the Huáscar is captured. Grau is revered as Peru's greatest naval hero.

sources.name: Jorge Ortiz Sotelo, 'El Almirante Miguel Grau' (2009)

events.title: Chilean Forces Occupy Lima
events.detail: Chilean forces defeat Peruvian defenses at the Battles of San Juan and Miraflores (January 13–15, 1881) and occupy Lima on January 17, 1881. Peruvian troops burn military depots and the National Library is looted. Chilean forces occupy Lima for over two years. President Piérola flees to the highlands, where guerrilla resistance (La Breña Campaign, led by Andrés Avelino Cáceres) continues until 1883. The war's devastation leaves Peru's economy in ruins for decades.

sources.name: Heraclio Bonilla, 'Guano y Burguesía en el Perú' (1974)

events.title: Treaty of Ancón Ends War of the Pacific
events.detail: Peru signs the Treaty of Ancón on October 20, 1884, ceding the Tarapacá region (rich in nitrates) to Chile permanently, and surrendering Tacna and Arica for ten years (with a plebiscite to determine their final status). The plebiscite is never held; Tacna is returned to Peru in 1929, while Arica remains Chilean. Bolivia loses its entire Pacific coast. The war's territorial and economic losses scar Peruvian national identity for generations, fueling anti-Chilean sentiment to the present day.

sources.name: Treaty of Ancón (1884), Archivo General de la Nación
Ronald Bruce St. John, 'The Foreign Policy of Peru' (1992)

events.title: Haya de la Torre Founds APRA
events.detail: Víctor Raúl Haya de la Torre founds the Alianza Popular Revolucionaria Americana (APRA) in Mexico City on May 7, 1924. APRA is the first mass political party in Peru, advocating anti-imperialism, nationalization of industry, unity of Indo-America, and protection of indigenous rights. It becomes the dominant political force in 20th-century Peru. Despite never winning the presidency until 1985 (due to military vetoes), APRA shapes Peruvian labor law, social policy, and political culture profoundly.

sources.name: Steve Stein, 'Populism in Peru: The Emergence of the Masses and the Politics of Social Control' (1980)
Víctor Raúl Haya de la Torre, collected works

media.source: Wikipedia / V%C3%ADctor Ra%C3%BAl Haya de la Torre

events.title: General Odría's Coup and the 'Ochenio'
events.detail: General Manuel Odría overthrows President José Luis Bustamante y Rivero on October 29, 1948, after Bustamante's government is paralyzed by APRA parliamentary obstruction and an abortive APRA military revolt. Odría's eight-year regime (the Ochenio, 1948–1956) suppresses APRA, jails political opponents, but benefits from a commodity boom and massive public works in Lima. Haya de la Torre takes asylum in the Colombian embassy for five years. Odría holds controlled elections in 1950 and eventually allows a return to civilian rule in 1956.

sources.name: Jorge Basadre, 'Historia de la República del Perú' (1964)

events.title: Fernando Belaúnde Terry's First Presidency
events.detail: Fernando Belaúnde Terry wins the 1963 election on a platform of agrarian reform, public works, and national integration. His government builds the Carretera Marginal de la Selva (Marginal Highway into the Amazon) but fails to pass meaningful land reform, blocked by an APRA-Odría congressional coalition. The IPC (International Petroleum Company) oil controversy, in which a signed agreement is declared fraudulent, creates a political crisis. On October 3, 1968, General Juan Velasco Alvarado overthrows him in a military coup.

media.source: Wikipedia / First presidency of Fernando Bela%C3%BAnde

events.title: Velasco's 'Revolutionary Government of the Armed Forces'
events.detail: General Juan Velasco Alvarado seizes power on October 3, 1968, in a bloodless coup against Belaúnde, citing the IPC oil scandal. Velasco leads what he calls a 'Revolutionary Government of the Armed Forces' — a nationalist, left-leaning military regime unlike any in Latin American history at the time. His government nationalizes IPC (Day 1), expropriates US and Peruvian oligarchic estates, establishes worker-managed industrial enterprises, and makes Quechua an official language. The military rules until 1980.

sources.name: Alfred Stepan, 'State and Society: Peru in Comparative Perspective' (1978)

events.title: Decree 17716: Velasco's Radical Agrarian Reform
events.detail: On June 24, 1969 (Día del Campesino), Velasco enacts Decree 17716 — the most sweeping agrarian reform in Latin American history to that date. The government expropriates all haciendas larger than 150 hectares on the coast and 55 hectares in the highlands, distributing roughly 9 million hectares to approximately 380,000 peasant families. The coastal sugar plantations become worker-owned cooperatives. While breaking up the landed oligarchy, the reform's cooperative model often fails, and agricultural productivity declines significantly in the following decade.

sources.name: Cynthia McClintock, 'Peasant Cooperatives and Political Change in Peru' (1981)

events.title: Morales Bermúdez Ousts Velasco; Reverses Reforms
events.detail: General Francisco Morales Bermúdez ousts the ailing Velasco in a bloodless 'corrective' coup on August 29, 1975. Morales begins rolling back Velasco's revolutionary reforms, opening Peru to IMF structural adjustment programs, restoring some expropriated assets, and suppressing labor unions. Facing economic crisis (inflation, debt), he calls a constituent assembly in 1978 and oversees the drafting of the 1979 constitution, allowing the 1980 election — Peru's first with full universal suffrage including illiterates.

sources.name: Julio Cotler, 'Clases, Estado y Nación en el Perú' (1978)

events.title: Shining Path Burns Ballots at Chuschi
events.detail: On May 17, 1980 — the night before Peru's first election with universal suffrage — members of Sendero Luminoso (Shining Path) burn ballot boxes in Chuschi, Ayacucho Department. The act, intended as a symbolic repudiation of bourgeois democracy, is initially dismissed as a minor incident. Founded by philosophy professor Abimael Guzmán ('Presidente Gonzalo') at the University of Huamanga, Sendero applies Mao Zedong Thought to an Andean context, aiming to encircle cities from the countryside and seize state power.

sources.name: Comisión de la Verdad y Reconciliación (CVR), Informe Final (2003)

events.title: Uchuraccay Massacre — 8 Journalists Killed
events.detail: On January 26, 1983, eight journalists traveling to investigate a massacre are killed by villagers in Uchuraccay, Ayacucho, who mistake them for Sendero Luminoso militants. A commission led by Nobel laureate Mario Vargas Llosa investigates. The incident exposes the extreme social isolation and fear of Andean communities caught between Sendero and the military. Vargas Llosa's report controversially attributes the killings to 'atavistic violence' of indigenous Andean communities, sparking intense debate about racism and Peru's deepening cultural fault lines.

sources.name: Informe de la Comisión Investigadora de los Sucesos de Uchuraccay (1983)

events.title: El Frontón Prison Massacre
events.detail: On June 18–19, 1986, Sendero Luminoso and MRTA prisoners stage coordinated uprisings in three Lima prisons (El Frontón island, Lurigancho, and Santa Bárbara) timed to coincide with a Socialist International congress in Lima. President Alan García orders military suppression. At El Frontón, Navy comandos detonate a wall and dynamite prisoners surrendering; at Lurigancho, Army troops execute survivors. CVR estimates 275 inmates killed, mostly after surrendering — constituting a major extrajudicial massacre by state forces.

sources.name: Amnesty International, 'Peru: Human Rights in a Climate of Terror' (1991)

events.title: Barrios Altos Massacre
events.detail: On November 3, 1991, a military death squad known as the Grupo Colina — operating under the orders of the National Intelligence Service (SIN) director Vladimiro Montesinos — opens fire at a neighborhood party in the Barrios Altos district of Lima, killing 15 civilians (including an eight-year-old child) and wounding four others. The operation targets an alleged Shining Path meeting. The massacre is later confirmed as a state crime; Fujimori and Montesinos are subsequently convicted for their role. It becomes a landmark case at the Inter-American Court of Human Rights.

sources.name: Inter-American Court of Human Rights, Barrios Altos v. Peru (2001)

events.title: La Cantuta University Massacre
events.detail: On July 18, 1992, the Grupo Colina death squad abducts nine students and one professor from La Cantuta National University in Lima. The victims are taken to a ravine in Cieneguilla, shot, and their bodies incinerated. For months the government denies any knowledge; the discovery of the burial site by opposition senator Roger Cáceres Velásquez using an anonymous map ('La Cantuta en la ruta de Colina') forces a partial acknowledgment. It becomes one of the defining symbols of state terrorism under Fujimori and is part of the crimes for which he is later convicted.

sources.name: Human Rights Watch, 'Peru: Anatomy of a Cover-Up' (1993)

events.title: Alberto Fujimori Elected President
events.detail: In a stunning political upset, Alberto Fujimori — a virtually unknown agricultural engineer and rector of the National Agrarian University — defeats novelist Mario Vargas Llosa in a June 10, 1990 runoff election. Running as 'El Chino' on the Cambio 90 platform, he appeals to Peru's poor, mestizo, and evangelical communities against Vargas Llosa's elite, pro-free-market FREDEMO coalition. Fujimori inherits hyperinflation (7,500% in 1990), a Shining Path insurgency at its peak, and near-state collapse.

sources.name: Jurado Nacional de Elecciones, Election Results 1990
Catherine Conaghan, 'Fujimori's Peru: Deception in the Public Sphere' (2005)

events.title: Fujimori's Autogolpe — Self-Coup
events.detail: On April 5, 1992, President Fujimori, backed by the military, dissolves Congress, suspends the constitution, closes the Constitutional Tribunal, and purges the judiciary in a televised address. He cites congressional obstruction of his anti-terrorism and anti-drug laws. The OAS condemns the action; most Western governments suspend aid. Despite international pressure, Fujimori retains high domestic approval (80%+) as Peruvians, exhausted by insurgency and economic collapse, support decisive action. He convenes a constituent assembly that drafts the 1993 constitution — still in force today.

events.title: Tarata Car Bomb Devastates Miraflores
events.detail: On July 16, 1992, Sendero Luminoso detonates two car bombs on Tarata Street in Miraflores — Lima's upscale commercial district — killing 25 people, injuring 155, and destroying 183 apartments and 400 businesses. The attack signals Sendero's campaign to terrorize Lima's middle class. It occurs just three months after Fujimori's autogolpe and two months before Guzmán's capture, demonstrating that Shining Path remained capable of urban mass attacks. It remains the deadliest terrorist bombing in Lima's history.

events.title: Capture of Abimael Guzmán
events.detail: On September 12, 1992, DINCOTE (National Counter-Terrorism Directorate) agents under General Ketin Vidal capture Abimael Guzmán — 'Presidente Gonzalo', founder and supreme leader of Shining Path — in a safe house in the Surquillo district of Lima. Guzmán is found hiding behind a false wall; agents follow clues including medicine for his psoriasis and used tubes of Chanel ointment in the garbage. His capture, without violence, is a massive intelligence success. Sendero enters an irreversible decline. Guzmán is tried by military tribunal and sentenced to life imprisonment.

sources.name: Gustavo Gorriti, 'The Shining Path: A History of the Millenarian War in Peru' (1990)

events.title: MRTA Seizes Japanese Embassy; Operation Chavín de Huántar
events.detail: On December 17, 1996, MRTA (Túpac Amaru Revolutionary Movement) militants storm the Japanese ambassador's residence during a reception, taking 72 hostages including ambassadors, ministers, and Fujimori's brother. After 126 days, on April 22, 1997, Peruvian commandos execute Operation Chavín de Huántar — tunneling under the residence and storming it. All 14 MRTA militants are killed (including at least 3 apparently shot after surrendering), all 72 hostages are freed, and 1 hostage and 2 commandos die. The operation boosts Fujimori's popularity dramatically.

sources.name: Inter-American Court of Human Rights, Cruz Sánchez v. Peru (2015)

events.title: Vladivideos Expose Montesinos — Government Collapses
events.detail: In September 2000, leaked videos ('vladivideos') filmed by Vladimiro Montesinos show him bribing congressmen, judges, media owners, and military officers. The revelations — broadcast on opposition cable TV channel Canal N — cause mass public outrage. Montesinos flees to Panama then Venezuela (where he is captured in 2001). Fujimori, attending an APEC summit in Brunei, announces he will call new elections and not be a candidate. In November 2000, he flies to Japan (his ancestral homeland) and resigns by fax. Congress votes to declare him 'morally unfit' rather than accept his resignation.

sources.name: Colección Vladivideos, Congreso de la República del Perú

events.title: Alejandro Toledo Elected — Peru's First Indigenous President
events.detail: Alejandro Toledo wins the June 2001 presidential election against Alan García, becoming Peru's first president of predominantly indigenous (Quechua) ancestry. His inauguration ceremony at Machu Picchu draws international attention. Toledo governs during Peru's strongest economic growth in decades (GDP growth averaging 5.6%), driven by commodity exports and macroeconomic stability. However, his administration is plagued by corruption scandals, low approval ratings (as low as 8%), and social conflicts over mining — including the Arequipazo protest (2002) and Ilave uprising (2004).

sources.name: Jurado Nacional de Elecciones, Election Results 2001

events.title: Truth and Reconciliation Commission Established
events.detail: On June 4, 2001, President Paniagua (interim) establishes the Comisión de la Verdad y Reconciliación (CVR) by Executive Decree 065-2001-PCM to investigate human rights violations committed between 1980 and 2000. Chaired by philosopher Salomón Lerner Febres, the CVR takes testimony from over 17,000 witnesses across Peru. Its two-year investigation constitutes the most comprehensive accounting of internal conflict deaths and atrocities in Latin American history.

events.title: CVR Final Report: 69,280 Deaths
events.detail: On August 28, 2003, the CVR presents its 9-volume, 12,000-page final report in Lima. Its statistical analysis — using multiple systems estimation — concludes that approximately 69,280 people were killed or disappeared during the 1980–2000 internal armed conflict. Shining Path is responsible for 54% of deaths; state forces (Army, police, paramilitaries) for 37%; MRTA and others for the remainder. The report finds that 75% of victims were Quechua-speaking peasants, exposing the conflict's deep racial and class dimensions. It calls for reparations, prosecutions, and institutional reforms.

events.title: Alan García Returns for Second Presidency
events.detail: Alan García of APRA wins the 2006 presidential election against Ollanta Humala, running as the moderate center-left alternative to Humala's left nationalism. His second term (2006–2011) oversees Peru's most sustained economic growth period — averaging 7% GDP growth annually, driven by metals exports. However, his government violently suppresses indigenous protesters opposing Amazon oil concessions at Bagua (June 5, 2009 — 'Baguazo'), killing 33 indigenous protesters and 24 police. He also presides over the height of the Odebrecht corruption era.

events.title: Fujimori Sentenced to 25 Years for Crimes Against Humanity
events.detail: On April 7, 2009, Peru's Supreme Court convicts Alberto Fujimori of crimes against humanity for the Barrios Altos and La Cantuta massacres and the Sótanos SIE kidnappings, sentencing him to 25 years in prison. The trial — conducted with full due process and televised — is celebrated internationally as a landmark accountability case for a former head of state. Fujimori later receives additional sentences for corruption. He dies in a Lima clinic on September 11, 2024, at age 86, without completing his sentence.

sources.name: Sala Penal Especial de la Corte Suprema, Exp. A.V. 19-2001 (2009)
Human Rights Watch, 'Peru: Fujimori Conviction Historic' (2009)

events.title: Ollanta Humala Wins Presidency
events.detail: Former military officer Ollanta Humala, previously a Hugo Chávez-style nationalist, wins the 2011 runoff against Keiko Fujimori by 51.4% to 48.6% after moderating his platform with the 'Hoja de Ruta' (roadmap), pledging to maintain macroeconomic stability while improving social inclusion. His presidency expands social programs (Pensión 65, Beca 18, Cuna Más), but is hobbled by falling commodity prices from 2013, growing social conflicts over mining, and corruption scandals affecting him and his wife Nadine Heredia.

events.title: Odebrecht Scandal Engulfs Peru's Entire Political Class
events.detail: Following the December 2016 US DOJ plea agreement with Odebrecht — the Brazilian construction giant — it emerges that the company paid over $29 million in bribes to Peruvian officials across multiple administrations to win infrastructure contracts. All four living former Peruvian presidents are investigated: Toledo (extradited from the US in 2023), García (commits suicide April 17, 2019, to avoid arrest), Humala (briefly jailed), and Kuczynski (house arrest). The scandal fundamentally delegitimizes Peru's post-2000 democratic political class.

events.title: Pedro Pablo Kuczynski Narrowly Wins Presidency
events.detail: Pedro Pablo Kuczynski (PPK), a 77-year-old Wall Street economist and former World Bank official, defeats Keiko Fujimori in the June 2016 runoff by 50.1% to 49.9% — the narrowest margin in Peruvian democratic history. His minority government faces immediate obstruction from Keiko Fujimori's Fuerza Popular, which holds 73 of 130 congressional seats. The political dynamic — an opposition majority Congress blocking an executive — defines the next six years of Peruvian politics.

events.title: PPK Pardons Fujimori in Secret Deal; Congress Erupts
events.detail: On December 24, 2017 — Christmas Eve — President Kuczynski issues a humanitarian pardon to Alberto Fujimori hours after Kenji Fujimori's congressional bloc saves PPK from impeachment by abstaining. Opposition legislators release 'mamanivideos' (videos of congressmen being offered public works in exchange for votes), exposing the deal. Mass protests erupt. The pardon is later revoked by the Constitutional Tribunal in October 2018, and Fujimori is returned to prison.

events.title: PPK Resigns; Vizcarra Becomes President
events.detail: On March 21, 2018, Kuczynski resigns the presidency after a second impeachment attempt exposes further 'mamanivideos' and Odebrecht connections. First Vice President Martín Vizcarra — governor of Moquegua, not implicated in Odebrecht — is sworn in on March 23. Vizcarra unexpectedly pivots to anti-corruption reform, proposing constitutional amendments and ultimately declaring a head-on confrontation with Congress's Fujimorista majority. His approval ratings reach 50–60%.

sources.name: Congreso de la República, Actas de sesión (2018)

events.title: Vizcarra Dissolves Congress; Calls New Elections
events.detail: On September 30, 2019, President Vizcarra invokes Article 134 of the Constitution and dissolves Congress after it grants confidence to a cabinet in bad faith to block an earlier dissolution motion. A constitutional debate erupts: Vizcarra claims this creates the condition for constitutional dissolution; Congress (briefly continuing as a 'rump parliament') declares him suspended and swears in Vice President Mercedes Aráoz. The Constitutional Tribunal upholds Vizcarra's dissolution. Extraordinary congressional elections in January 2020 produce a fragmented new Congress.

events.title: Vizcarra Impeached; Merino Resigns After Six Days
events.detail: On November 9, 2020, Congress impeaches Vizcarra on 'moral incapacity' grounds related to construction contracts in Moquegua, by 105–19 votes. Congressional president Manuel Merino is sworn in as president. Mass protests (the 'segunda ola' — second wave) erupt nationwide; two protesters (Inti Sotelo and Bryan Pintado) are killed by police. Merino resigns after just six days on November 15, 2020. Francisco Sagasti, a centrist, becomes Peru's fourth president in five years, stabilizing the situation through the remainder of the term.

sources.name: Congreso de la República del Perú, Diario de Debates (2020)

events.title: Pedro Castillo Elected — Peru's Most Unexpected President
events.detail: Pedro Castillo, a rural schoolteacher and union leader from Cajamarca with no prior electoral experience, wins the April–June 2021 presidential election, defeating Keiko Fujimori by 50.1% to 49.8% — just 44,000 votes out of 18 million cast. Castillo's Perú Libre party, openly Marxist-Leninist, mobilizes Peru's rural interior. Keiko Fujimori alleges fraud for weeks; election authorities certify Castillo's victory on July 19. Castillo appoints seven cabinets in 17 months, is investigated for corruption, and proves unable to govern effectively.

events.title: Castillo's Failed Self-Coup; Boluarte Becomes President
events.detail: On December 7, 2022, facing his third impeachment attempt and a corruption investigation, President Castillo makes a televised address declaring a curfew, dissolving Congress, and announcing a 'government of exception.' The announcement is immediately rejected by the cabinet, the attorney general, the Constitutional Tribunal, and the military. Congress votes 101–47 to impeach him; Castillo is arrested while attempting to reach the Mexican embassy. First Vice President Dina Boluarte is sworn in as Peru's first female president — the sixth president in four years.

sources.name: Congreso de la República del Perú, Diario de Debates, December 7, 2022

events.title: Anti-Boluarte Protests: 70+ Deaths in Andean Crackdown
events.detail: Between December 2022 and March 2023, mass protests erupted across Peru — centered in Ayacucho, Puno, Arequipa, Cusco, and Apurímac — demanding Boluarte's resignation, Castillo's release, dissolution of Congress, and a constituent assembly. Security forces fire live ammunition; the Defensoría del Pueblo records 60–70+ deaths, mostly in Andean communities. The Inter-American Commission on Human Rights condemns the response. Puno (department) accounts for approximately 18 deaths in a single week. Boluarte declares three states of emergency and accuses Castillo's supporters of sedition.

sources.name: Defensoría del Pueblo, Informes de Derechos Humanos (2023)

events.title: Alberto Fujimori Dies at Age 86
events.detail: Alberto Fujimori dies on September 11, 2024 — a date heavy with symbolism — at a Lima clinic, aged 86, from tongue cancer. He spent his final years under house arrest after a brief and contested presidential pardon (granted December 2023 by the Constitutional Tribunal on humanitarian grounds) overrode the Supreme Court's earlier pardon revocation. His death triggers both mourning processions by supporters and protests by human rights groups. The debate over his legacy — economic stabilizer and Shining Path defeater vs. authoritarian criminal convicted of crimes against humanity — remains sharply contested in Peru.
